The **English Civil War** or **Great Rebellion** was a series of [civil wars](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war") and political machinations between [Royalists](/wiki/Cavaliers "Cavaliers") and [Parliamentarians](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") in the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England")[[b]](#cite_note-4) from 1642 to 1651. Part of the wider 1639 to 1653 [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms"), the struggle consisted of the [First English Civil War](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War "First English Civil War") and the [Second English Civil War](/wiki/Second_English_Civil_War "Second English Civil War"). The [Anglo-Scottish war](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_war_(1650%E2%80%931652) "Anglo-Scottish war (1650–1652)") of 1650 to 1652 is sometimes referred to as the *Third English Civil War.*

While the conflicts in the three kingdoms of England, [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") had similarities, each had its own specific issues and objectives. The First English Civil War was fought primarily over the correct balance of power between [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"). It ended in June 1646 with Royalist defeat and the king in custody.

However, victory exposed Parliamentarian divisions over the nature of the political settlement. The vast majority went to war in 1642 to assert Parliament's right to participate in government, not abolish the monarchy, which meant Charles' refusal to make concessions led to a stalemate. Concern over the political influence of radicals within the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army") like [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") led to an alliance between moderate Parliamentarians and Royalists, supported by the [Covenanter Scots](/wiki/Covenanters "Covenanters"). Royalist defeat in the 1648 Second English Civil War resulted in the [execution of Charles I](/wiki/Execution_of_Charles_I "Execution of Charles I") in January 1649, and the establishment of the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England").

In 1650, [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") was crowned [King of Scotland](/wiki/King_of_Scotland "King of Scotland"), in return for agreeing to create a [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") church in both England and Scotland. The subsequent Anglo-Scottish war ended with Parliamentarian victory at [Worcester](/wiki/Battle_of_Worcester "Battle of Worcester") on 3 September 1651. Both Ireland and Scotland were incorporated into the Commonwealth, and the British Isles became a unitary state. This arrangement ultimately proved both unpopular and unviable in the long term, and was dissolved upon the [Stuart Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration") in 1660. The outcome of the civil wars effectively set England and Scotland on course towards a [parliamentary monarchy](/wiki/Parliamentary_monarchy "Parliamentary monarchy") form of government.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The term *English Civil War* appears most often in the singular, but historians often divide the conflict into two or three separate wars. These were not restricted to [England](/wiki/England "England") alone, as [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") (having been [annexed](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535_and_1542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542") into the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England")) was affected by the same political instabilities. The conflicts also involved wars with [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") and civil wars within them. Some historians have favoured the term *The British Civil Wars*. From the Restoration to the 19th century, the common phrase for the civil wars was "the rebellion" or "the great rebellion".[[3]](#cite_note-5)

The wars spanning all four countries are known as the [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms"). In the early 19th century, [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott") referred to it as "The Great Civil War".[[4]](#cite_note-6) The [1911 *Encyclopædia Britannica*](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition "Encyclopædia Britannica Eleventh Edition") called the series of conflicts the "Great Rebellion".[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-7) Some historians, notably [Marxists](/wiki/Marxists "Marxists") such as [Christopher Hill](/wiki/Christopher_Hill_(historian) "Christopher Hill (historian)"), favoured the term "[English Revolution](/wiki/English_Revolution "English Revolution")".[[6]](#cite_note-8)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Each side had a geographical stronghold, such that minority elements were silenced or fled. The Royalist areas included the countryside, [the Shires](/wiki/Shire#"The_Shires" "Shire"), the cathedral cities of [York](/wiki/York "York"), [Chester](/wiki/Chester "Chester"), [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England") and [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford"), and the less economically developed areas of northern and western England and Wales. Parliament's strengths spanned the industrial centres, ports, and economically advanced regions of southern and eastern England, including the remaining cathedral cities. Historian [Lacey Baldwin Smith](/wiki/Lacey_Baldwin_Smith "Lacey Baldwin Smith") wrote: "the words *populous, rich, and rebellious* seemed to go hand in hand".[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1983251-9)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHughes1985236–263-10)

## Strategy and tactics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Strategy and tactics")]

Many officers and veteran soldiers had fought in European wars, notably the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") between the Spanish and the Dutch, which began in 1568, as well as earlier phases of the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") which began in 1618 and concluded in 1648.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaker1986-11)

The war was of unprecedented scale for the English. During the campaign seasons, 120,000 to 150,000 soldiers would be in the field, a higher proportion of the population than were fighting in Germany in the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War").[[10]](#cite_note-12)

The main battle tactic came to be known as [pike and shot](/wiki/Pike_and_shot "Pike and shot") infantry. The two sides would line up opposite one another, with infantry brigades of [musketeers](/wiki/Musketeer "Musketeer") in the centre. These carried matchlock muskets, an inaccurate weapon which nevertheless could be lethal at a range of up to 300 yards. Musketeers would assemble three rows deep, the first kneeling, second crouching, and third standing. At times, troops divided into two groups, allowing one to reload while the other fired.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurneYoung1998-13)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Among the musketeers were pike men, carrying [pikes](/wiki/Pike_(weapon) "Pike (weapon)") of 12 feet (4 m) to 18 feet (5.5 m) long, whose main purpose was to protect the musketeers from cavalry charges. Positioned on each side of the infantry were cavalry, with a right wing led by the [lieutenant-general](/wiki/Lieutenant_general "Lieutenant general") and the left by the [commissary general](/wiki/Commissary_general "Commissary general"). Its main aim was to rout the opponents' cavalry, then turn and overpower their infantry.[[12]](#cite_note-Simkin-14)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[13]](#cite_note-15)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The Royalist cavaliers' skill and speed on horseback led to many early victories. [Prince Rupert](/wiki/Prince_Rupert_of_the_Rhine "Prince Rupert of the Rhine"), commanding the king's cavalry, used a tactic learned while fighting in the Dutch army, where cavalry would charge at full speed into the opponent's infantry, firing their pistols just before impact.[[12]](#cite_note-Simkin-14)[[14]](#cite_note-16)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

However, with Oliver Cromwell and the introduction of the more disciplined [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army"), a group of disciplined pike men would stand its ground, which could have a devastating effect. The Royalist cavalry had a tendency to chase down individual targets after the initial charge, leaving their forces scattered and tired, whereas [Cromwell's cavalry](/wiki/Ironside_(cavalry) "Ironside (cavalry)") was slower but better disciplined.[[12]](#cite_note-Simkin-14) Trained to operate as a single unit, it went on to win many decisive victories.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Background")]

The English Civil War broke out in 1642, less than 40 years after the death of Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England"). Elizabeth had been succeeded by her [first cousin twice-removed](/wiki/Cousin_chart "Cousin chart"), [King James VI of Scotland](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), as James I of England, creating the first [personal union of the Scottish and English kingdoms](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns").[[c]](#cite_note-18) As King of Scots, James had become accustomed to Scotland's weak parliamentary tradition since assuming control of the Scottish government in 1583, so that upon assuming power south of the border, the new King of England was affronted by the constraints the [English Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") attempted to place on him in exchange for money. Consequently, James's personal extravagance, which resulted in him being perennially short of money, meant that he had to resort to extra-parliamentary sources of income. Moreover, increasing inflation during this period meant that even though Parliament was granting the King the same nominal value of subsidy, the income was actually worth less.[[16]](#cite_note-19)

This extravagance was tempered by James's peaceful disposition, so that by the succession of his son Charles I in 1625 the two kingdoms had both experienced relative peace, internally and in their relations with each other. Charles followed his father's dream in hoping to unite the kingdoms of England, Scotland and Ireland into a single kingdom.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECroft200363-20) Many English Parliamentarians were suspicious of such a move, fearing that such a new kingdom might destroy old English traditions that had bound the English monarchy.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Because James had described kings as "little gods on Earth", chosen by God to rule in accordance with the doctrine of the [Divine Right of Kings](/wiki/Divine_Right_of_Kings "Divine Right of Kings"), and Charles shared his father's position, the suspicions of the Parliamentarians had some justification.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcClelland1996224-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/King_Charles_I_after_original_by_van_Dyck.jpg/330px-King_Charles_I_after_original_by_van_Dyck.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Charles_I_after_original_by_van_Dyck.jpg)

[Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") believed in the [divine right of kings](/wiki/Divine_right_of_kings "Divine right of kings"); painting by [Van Dyck](/wiki/Anthony_van_Dyck "Anthony van Dyck")

#### Parliament in an English constitutional framework

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Parliament in an English constitutional framework")]

At the time, the Parliament of England did not have a large permanent role in the English system of government. Instead, it functioned as a temporary advisory committee and was summoned only if and when the monarch saw fit. Once summoned, a Parliament's continued existence was at the King's pleasure since it was subject to dissolution by him at any time.

Yet in spite of this limited role, Parliament had acquired over the centuries *de facto* powers of enough significance that monarchs could not simply ignore them indefinitely. For a monarch, Parliament's most indispensable power was its ability to raise tax revenues far in excess of all other sources of revenue at the Crown's disposal. By the 17th century, Parliament's tax-raising powers had come to be derived from the fact that the [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry") was the only stratum of society with the ability and authority to collect and remit the most meaningful forms of taxation then available at the local level. So, if the king wanted to ensure smooth revenue collection, he needed the gentry's cooperation. For all of the Crown's legal authority, its resources were limited by any modern standard to the extent that if the gentry refused to collect the king's taxes on a national scale, the Crown lacked a practical means of compelling them.

From the thirteenth century, monarchs ordered the election of representatives to sit in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England"), with most voters being the owners of property, although in some [potwalloper](/wiki/Potwalloper "Potwalloper") boroughs every male householder could vote. When assembled along with the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), these elected representatives formed a Parliament. So the concept of Parliaments allowed representatives of the property-owning class to meet, primarily, at least from the point of view of the monarch, to sanction whatever taxes the monarch wished to collect. In the process, the representatives could debate and enact [statutes](/wiki/Statute "Statute"), or [acts](/wiki/Act_of_parliament "Act of parliament"). However, Parliament lacked the power to force its will upon the monarch; its only leverage was the threat of withholding the financial means required to implement his plans.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnston190183–86-22)

#### Parliamentary concerns and the Petition of Right

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Parliamentary concerns and the Petition of Right")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Sir_Peter_Lely_001.jpg/250px-Sir_Peter_Lely_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Peter_Lely_001.jpg)

[Henrietta Maria](/wiki/Henrietta_Maria_of_France "Henrietta Maria of France"), painted by [Peter Lely](/wiki/Peter_Lely "Peter Lely"), 1660

Many concerns were raised over Charles's marriage in 1625 to a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") French princess, [Henrietta Maria](/wiki/Henrietta_Maria_of_France "Henrietta Maria of France"). Parliament refused to assign him the traditional right to collect customs duties for his entire reign, deciding instead to grant it only on a provisional basis and negotiate with him.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregg1984129–130-23)

Charles, meanwhile, decided to send an expeditionary force to relieve the French [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots"), whom French royal troops held [besieged in La Rochelle](/wiki/Siege_of_La_Rochelle "Siege of La Rochelle"). Such military support for Protestants on the Continent potentially alleviated concerns about the King's marriage to a Catholic. However, Charles's insistence on giving command of the English force to his unpopular royal favorite [George Villiers, the Duke of Buckingham](/wiki/George_Villiers,_1st_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham"), undermined that support. Unfortunately for Charles and Buckingham, the 1627 relief expedition proved a fiasco,[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregg1984166-24) and Parliament, already hostile to Buckingham for his monopoly on [royal patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage"), opened [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment_in_the_United_Kingdom "Impeachment in the United Kingdom") proceedings against him.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregg1984175-25) Charles responded by dissolving Parliament. This saved Buckingham but confirmed the impression that Charles wanted to avoid Parliamentary scrutiny of his ministers.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregg1984175-25)

Having dissolved Parliament and unable to raise money without it, the king assembled a new one in 1628. (The elected members included [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), [John Hampden](/wiki/John_Hampden "John Hampden"),[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdair1976-26) and [Edward Coke](/wiki/Edward_Coke "Edward Coke").) The new Parliament drew up a [Petition of Right](/wiki/Petition_of_Right "Petition of Right"), which Charles accepted as a concession to obtain his subsidy.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss200793-27) The Petition made reference to [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta"),[[25]](#cite_note-28) but did not grant him the right of [tonnage and poundage](/wiki/Tonnage_and_poundage "Tonnage and poundage"), which Charles had been collecting without Parliamentary authorization since 1625.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommerville199265,_71,_80-29) Several more active members of the opposition were imprisoned, which caused outrage;[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommerville199265,_71,_80-29) one, [John Eliot](/wiki/John_Eliot_(statesman) "John Eliot (statesman)"), subsequently died in prison and came to be seen as a martyr for the rights of Parliament.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussell1998417-30)

#### Personal rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal rule")]

Charles avoided calling a Parliament for the next decade, a period known as the "[personal rule of Charles I](/wiki/Personal_rule_of_Charles_I "Personal rule of Charles I")", or by its critics as the "Eleven Years' Tyranny".[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosnerTheibault2000103-31) During this period, Charles's policies were determined by his lack of money. First and foremost, to avoid Parliament, the King needed to avoid war. Charles made peace with France and Spain, effectively ending England's involvement in the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"). However, that in itself was far from enough to balance the Crown's finances.

Unable to raise revenue without Parliament and unwilling to convene it, Charles resorted to other means. One was to revive conventions, often outdated. For example, a failure to attend and receive [knighthood](/wiki/Knighthood "Knighthood") at Charles's coronation became a finable offence with the fine paid to the Crown. The King also tried to raise revenue through [ship money](/wiki/Ship_money "Ship money"), demanding in 1634–1636 that the inland English counties pay a tax for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") to counter the threat of privateers and pirates in the English Channel.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdair1976-26) Established law supported the policy of coastal counties and inland ports such as London paying ship money in times of need, but it had not been applied to inland counties before.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdair1976-26)

Authorities had ignored it for centuries, and many saw it as yet another extra-Parliamentary, illegal tax,[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPipes1999143-32) which prompted some prominent men to refuse to pay it. Charles issued a writ against [John Hampden](/wiki/John_Hampden "John Hampden") for his failure to pay, and although five judges including George Croke supported Hampden, seven judges found in favour of the King in 1638.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdair1976-26) The fines imposed on people who refused to pay ship money and standing out against its illegality aroused widespread indignation.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPipes1999143-32)

During his "Personal Rule", Charles aroused most antagonism through his religious measures. He believed in [High Anglicanism](/wiki/High_church "High church"), a sacramental version of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), theologically based upon [Arminianism](/wiki/Arminianism_in_the_Church_of_England "Arminianism in the Church of England"), a creed shared with his main political adviser, Archbishop [William Laud](/wiki/William_Laud "William Laud").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton198748-33) In 1633, Charles appointed Laud [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury") and started making the Church more ceremonial, replacing the wooden [communion](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy)#Communion_rite "Mass (liturgy)") tables with stone altars.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton198796-34) [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") accused Laud of reintroducing Catholicism, and when they complained he had them arrested. In 1637, [John Bastwick](/wiki/John_Bastwick "John Bastwick"), [Henry Burton](/wiki/Henry_Burton_(Puritan) "Henry Burton (Puritan)"), and [William Prynne](/wiki/William_Prynne "William Prynne") had their ears cut off for writing pamphlets attacking Laud's views – a rare penalty for [gentlemen](/wiki/Gentleman "Gentleman"), and one that aroused anger.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss2007201-35) Moreover, the Church authorities revived statutes from the time of Elizabeth I about church attendance and fined Puritans for not attending Anglican services.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1987173-36)

#### Rebellion in Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Rebellion in Scotland")]

Main article: [Bishops' Wars](/wiki/Bishops%27_Wars "Bishops' Wars")

The end of Charles's independent governance came when he attempted to apply the same religious policies in Scotland. The [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"), reluctantly [episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") in structure, had independent traditions.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss200774-37) Charles wanted one uniform Church throughout Britain[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss200783-38) and introduced a new, High Anglican version of the English [Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer") to Scotland in the middle of 1637. This was violently resisted. A riot broke out in Edinburgh,[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss200775-39) which may have been started in [St Giles' Cathedral](/wiki/St_Giles%27_Cathedral "St Giles' Cathedral"), according to legend, by [Jenny Geddes](/wiki/Jenny_Geddes "Jenny Geddes"). In February 1638, the Scots formulated their objections to royal policy in the [National Covenant](/wiki/National_Covenant "National Covenant").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss200777-40) This document took the form of a "loyal protest", rejecting all innovations not first tested by free Parliaments and General Assemblies of the Church.

By the spring of 1639, a political movement known as the [Covenanters](/wiki/Covenanters "Covenanters") had taken control of Scotland and Charles decided to re-assert his authority by force. He accompanied an army to the Scottish border near [Berwick](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed") but hostilities were avoided when [a truce was agreed](/wiki/Treaty_of_Berwick_(1639) "Treaty of Berwick (1639)"). However, both sides viewed the truce as temporary, and continued preparations for military confrontation.

#### Recall of the English Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Recall of the English Parliament")]

Main article: [Short Parliament](/wiki/Short_Parliament "Short Parliament")

Charles needed to suppress the rebellion in Scotland but he lacked sufficient funds. To obtain finance, in February 1640 for the first time in 11 years, he recalled an English Parliament.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoward1994180-41) The majority faction of the new Parliament, led by [John Pym](/wiki/John_Pym "John Pym"), were opposed to an English invasion of Scotland and used Charles's appeal for money as a chance to redress grievances. Charles took exception to this *[lèse-majesté](/wiki/Lese_majesty "Lese majesty")* (offence against the ruler). Negotiations collapsed and after only a few weeks and he dissolved what later became known as the [Short Parliament](/wiki/Short_Parliament "Short Parliament").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoward1994180-41)

Without Parliament's support, Charles again prepared for war with Scotland. In August, a Scots army invaded, won a battle at [Newburn](/wiki/Battle_of_Newburn "Battle of Newburn") and occupied parts of the north of England.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoward1994180-41) This put Charles in a desperate financial state and according to advice from the [Magnum Concilium](/wiki/Magnum_Concilium "Magnum Concilium") (the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), but without the [Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England")) [another truce was established](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ripon "Treaty of Ripon") where Charles agreed to pay the Scottish army's expenses pending the negotiation of a final settlement. He also agreed to summon another English Parliament in November 1640.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss200796-42)

#### The Long Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: The Long Parliament")]

Main article: [Long Parliament](/wiki/Long_Parliament "Long Parliament")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Platform_of_the_Lower_House_of_this_Present_Parliament_%28of_England%29_Assembled_at_Westminster_1640.jpg/500px-Platform_of_the_Lower_House_of_this_Present_Parliament_%28of_England%29_Assembled_at_Westminster_1640.jpg)](/wiki/File:Platform_of_the_Lower_House_of_this_Present_Parliament_(of_England)_Assembled_at_Westminster_1640.jpg)

A sitting of the [Long Parliament](/wiki/Long_Parliament "Long Parliament"), 1640

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/89/Attempted_Arrest_of_the_Five_members_by_Charles_West_Cope.jpg)](/wiki/File:Attempted_Arrest_of_the_Five_members_by_Charles_West_Cope.jpg)

The king's attempt to arrest the [Five Members](/wiki/Five_Members "Five Members"), depicted in a 19th-century painting.

The new Parliament proved even more hostile to Charles than its predecessor. It immediately began to discuss grievances against him and his government, with Pym and [Hampden](/wiki/John_Hampden "John Hampden") in the lead. They took the opportunity presented by the King's troubles to force various reforming measures – including many with strong "anti-[Papist](/wiki/Popery "Popery")" themes – upon him.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss2007104–105-43) The members passed a law stating that a new Parliament would convene at least once every three years – without the King's summons if need be. Other laws passed making it illegal for the king to impose taxes without Parliamentary consent and later gave Parliament control over the King's ministers.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss2007104–105-43)

Finally, the Parliament passed a law forbidding the King to dissolve it without its consent, even if the three years were up. These laws equated to a tremendous increase in Parliamentary power. Ever since, this Parliament has been known as the [Long Parliament](/wiki/Long_Parliament "Long Parliament"). However, Parliament did attempt to avert conflict by requiring all adults to sign [The Protestation](/wiki/Protestation_of_1641 "Protestation of 1641"), an oath of allegiance to Charles.[[d]](#cite_note-44)

Early in the Long Parliament, the house overwhelmingly accused [Thomas Wentworth](/wiki/Thomas_Wentworth,_1st_Earl_of_Strafford "Thomas Wentworth, 1st Earl of Strafford") (now Earl of Strafford) of high treason and other crimes and misdemeanors. [Henry Vane the Younger](/wiki/Henry_Vane_the_Younger "Henry Vane the Younger") supplied evidence of Strafford's claimed improper use of the army in Ireland, alleging that he had encouraged the King to use his Ireland-raised forces to threaten England into compliance. This evidence was obtained from Vane's father, [Henry Vane the Elder](/wiki/Henry_Vane_the_Elder "Henry Vane the Elder"), a member of the King's Privy Council, who refused to confirm it in Parliament out of loyalty to Charles. On 10 April 1641, Pym's case collapsed, but Pym made a direct appeal to the Younger Vane to produce a copy of the notes from the King's Privy Council, discovered by the Younger Vane and secretly turned over to Pym, to the great anguish of the Elder Vane.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpham2009187-45) These notes contained evidence that Strafford had told the King, "Sir, you have done your duty, and your subjects have failed in theirs; and therefore you are absolved from the rules of government, and may supply yourself by extraordinary ways; you have an army in Ireland, with which you may reduce the kingdom."[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpham2009187-45)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHibbert1968154-46)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1995224-47)

Pym immediately launched a [Bill of Attainder](/wiki/Bill_of_attainder "Bill of attainder") stating Strafford's guilt and demanding that he be put to death.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1995224-47) Unlike a guilty verdict in a court case, attainder did not require a [legal burden of proof](/wiki/Legal_burden_of_proof "Legal burden of proof") to be met, but it did require the king's approval. Charles, however, guaranteed Strafford that he would not sign the attainder, without which the bill could not be passed.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1995225-48) Furthermore, the Lords opposed the severity of a death sentence on Strafford. Yet increased tensions and [a plot in the army](/wiki/First_Army_Plot "First Army Plot") to support Strafford began to sway the issue.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1995225-48)

On 21 April, the Commons passed the Bill (204 in favour, 59 opposed, and 250 abstained),[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1999123-49) and the Lords acquiesced. Charles, still incensed over the Commons' handling of Buckingham, refused his assent. Strafford himself, hoping to head off the war he saw looming, wrote to the king and asked him to reconsider.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbott2020-50) Charles, fearing for the safety of his family, signed on 10 May.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith1999123-49) Strafford was beheaded two days later.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoward1994191-51) In the meantime, both Parliament and the King agreed to an independent investigation into the king's involvement in Strafford's plot.

The Long Parliament then passed the [Triennial Act 1640](/wiki/Triennial_Act_1640 "Triennial Act 1640"), also known as the Dissolution Act, in May 1641, to which [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") was readily granted.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1995222-52)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyon1978127-53) The Triennial Act required Parliament to be summoned at least once in three years. When the king failed to issue a proper summons, the members could assemble on their own. This act also forbade ship money without Parliament's consent, fines in distraint of knighthood, and forced loans. Monopolies were cut back sharply, the courts of the [Star Chamber](/wiki/Star_Chamber "Star Chamber") and [High Commission](/wiki/Court_of_High_Commission "Court of High Commission") abolished by the [Habeas Corpus Act 1640](/wiki/Habeas_Corpus_Act_1640 "Habeas Corpus Act 1640"), and the Triennial Act respectively.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregg1981335-54)

All remaining forms of taxation were legalised and regulated by the [Tonnage and Poundage Act 1640](/wiki/Tonnage_and_Poundage_Act_1640 "Tonnage and Poundage Act 1640").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyon1978129-55) On 3 May, Parliament decreed [The Protestation](/wiki/Protestation_of_1641 "Protestation of 1641"), attacking the 'wicked counsels' of Charles's government, whereby those who signed the petition undertook to defend 'the true reformed religion', Parliament, and the king's person, honour and estate. Throughout May, the House of Commons launched several bills attacking bishops and Episcopalianism in general, each time defeated in the Lords.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyon1978130-56)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbott2020-50)

Charles and his Parliament hoped that the execution of Strafford and the Protestation would end the drift towards war, but in fact, they encouraged it. Charles and his supporters continued to resent Parliament's demands, and Parliamentarians continued to suspect Charles of wanting to impose [episcopalianism](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") and unfettered royal rule by military force. Within months, the Irish Catholics, fearing a resurgence of Protestant power, [struck first](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1641 "Irish Rebellion of 1641"), and all Ireland soon descended into chaos.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss2007109–113-57) Rumours circulated that the King supported the Irish, and Puritan members of the Commons soon started murmuring that this exemplified the fate that Charles had in store for them all.[[54]](#cite_note-58)

On 4 January 1642, Charles, followed by 400 soldiers, entered the House of Commons and attempted to arrest [five members](/wiki/Five_members "Five members") on a charge of treason.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherwood199741-59) The members had learned that he was coming and escaped. Charles not only failed to arrest them but turned more people against him.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHibbert199332-60)

#### Local grievances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Local grievances")]

In the summer of 1642, these national troubles helped to polarise opinion, ending indecision about which side to support or what action to take. Opposition to Charles also arose from many local grievances. For example, imposed drainage schemes in [The Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens") disrupted the livelihood of thousands after the King awarded a number of drainage contracts.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHughes1991127-61) Many saw the King as indifferent to public welfare, and this played a role in bringing much of eastern England into the Parliamentarian camp. This sentiment brought with it such people as [Edward Montagu, Earl of Manchester](/wiki/Edward_Montagu,_2nd_Earl_of_Manchester "Edward Montagu, 2nd Earl of Manchester") and [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), each a notable wartime adversary of the King. Conversely, one of the leading drainage contractors, [Robert Bertie, Earl of Lindsey](/wiki/Robert_Bertie,_1st_Earl_of_Lindsey "Robert Bertie, 1st Earl of Lindsey"), was to die fighting for the King at the [Battle of Edgehill](/wiki/Battle_of_Edgehill "Battle of Edgehill").[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPurkiss2007180-62)

### First English Civil War (1642–1646)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: First English Civil War (1642–1646)")]

Main article: [First English Civil War](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War "First English Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/English_civil_war_map_1642_to_1645.JPG/250px-English_civil_war_map_1642_to_1645.JPG)](/wiki/File:English_civil_war_map_1642_to_1645.JPG)

Maps of territory held by Royalists (red) and Parliamentarians (yellow-green), 1642–1645

In early January 1642, a few days after failing to capture five members of the House of Commons, Charles feared for the safety of his family and retinue and left the London area for the north country.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood197057-63)

Further frequent negotiations by letter between the King and the Long Parliament, through to early summer, proved fruitless. On 1 June[[60]](#cite_note-64) the [English Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") and [Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England") approved a list of proposals known as the [Nineteen Propositions](/wiki/Nineteen_Propositions "Nineteen Propositions"). In these demands, the Parliament sought a larger share of power in the governance of the kingdom. Before the end of the month the King rejected the Propositions.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Cavalier_Troops_Mustering_outside_the_Guildhall%2C_Exeter.jpg/330px-Cavalier_Troops_Mustering_outside_the_Guildhall%2C_Exeter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cavalier_Troops_Mustering_outside_the_Guildhall,_Exeter.jpg)

'Cavalier Troops Mustering outside the Guildhall, Exeter' by John Joseph Barker, 1886. This oil on canvas depicts Cavalier troops outside the [Exeter Guildhall](/wiki/Exeter_Guildhall "Exeter Guildhall"). From the [Royal Albert Memorial Museum](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Memorial_Museum "Royal Albert Memorial Museum")'s collection (93/1978x)

As the summer progressed, cities and towns declared their sympathies for one faction or the other: for example, the garrison of [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") commanded by [George Goring](/wiki/George_Goring,_Lord_Goring "George Goring, Lord Goring") declared for the King,[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970107-65) but when Charles tried to acquire arms from [Kingston upon Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull"), the weaponry depository used in the previous Scottish campaigns, [John Hotham](/wiki/Sir_John_Hotham,_1st_Baronet "Sir John Hotham, 1st Baronet"), the military governor appointed by Parliament in January, refused to let Charles enter the town,[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood197082-66) and when Charles returned with more men later, [Hotham drove them off](/wiki/Siege_of_Hull_(1642) "Siege of Hull (1642)").[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970100-67) Charles issued a warrant for Hotham's arrest as a traitor but was powerless to enforce it. Throughout the summer, tensions rose and there was brawling in several places, the first death from the conflict taking place in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester").[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970100-67)[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyle2006158–166-68)

#### 1642 battles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 1642 battles")]

Main article: [First English Civil War, 1642](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War,_1642 "First English Civil War, 1642")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Oliver_Cromwell_by_Samuel_Cooper.jpg/330px-Oliver_Cromwell_by_Samuel_Cooper.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oliver_Cromwell_by_Samuel_Cooper.jpg)

[Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") rose to become  
[Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector") in 1653

At the outset of the conflict, much of the country remained neutral, though the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") and most English cities favoured Parliament, while the King found marked support in rural communities. The war quickly spread and eventually involved every level of society. Many areas attempted to remain neutral. Some formed bands of [Clubmen](/wiki/Clubmen "Clubmen") to protect their localities from the worst excesses of the armies of both sides,[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970403–404-69) but most found it impossible to withstand both King and Parliament.

On one side, the King and his supporters fought for what they saw as traditional government in church and state. On the other, most Parliamentarians initially took up arms to defend what they viewed as a traditional balance of government in church and state, and which they felt had been undermined by bad advice the King received from his advisers — such as [George Villiers, Duke of Buckingham](/wiki/George_Villiers,_1st_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham") — and during his [Personal Rule](/wiki/Personal_Rule "Personal Rule") (the "Eleven Years' Tyranny"). The views of the members of Parliament ranged from unquestioning support of the King – at one point during the First Civil War, more members of the Commons and Lords gathered in the King's [Oxford Parliament](/wiki/Oxford_Parliament_(1644) "Oxford Parliament (1644)") than at [Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") — through to radicals who sought major reforms in [religious independence](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") and redistribution of power at a national level.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/The_great_civil_war_of_the_times_of_Charles_I._and_Cromwell_%281857%29_%2814576032568%29.jpg/250px-The_great_civil_war_of_the_times_of_Charles_I._and_Cromwell_%281857%29_%2814576032568%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_great_civil_war_of_the_times_of_Charles_I._and_Cromwell_(1857)_(14576032568).jpg)

Charles I. and Cromwell

After the debacle at Hull, Charles moved on to [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham "Nottingham"), raising the royal standard there on 22 August 1642.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970111-70) At the time, Charles had with him about 2,000 cavalry and a small number of Yorkshire infantrymen, and using the archaic system of a [Commission of Array](/wiki/Commission_of_Array "Commission of Array"),[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood197096-71) his supporters started to build a larger army around the standard. Charles moved in a westerly direction, first to [Stafford](/wiki/Stafford "Stafford"), then on to [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Shrewsbury "Shrewsbury"), as support for his cause seemed particularly strong in the [Severn](/wiki/River_Severn "River Severn") valley area and in North Wales.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyle2006170,_183-72) While passing through [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington,_Shropshire "Wellington, Shropshire"), he declared in what became known as the "[Wellington Declaration](/wiki/Wellington_Declaration "Wellington Declaration")" that he would uphold the "Protestant religion, the laws of England, and the liberty of Parliament".[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherwood19926-73)

The Parliamentarians who opposed the King did not remain passive in this pre-war period. As in Hull, they took measures to secure strategic towns and cities by appointing to office men sympathetic to their cause. On 9 June they voted to raise an army of 10,000 volunteers and appointed [Robert Devereux, 3rd Earl of Essex](/wiki/Robert_Devereux,_3rd_Earl_of_Essex "Robert Devereux, 3rd Earl of Essex") its commander three days later.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970108–109-74) He received orders "to rescue His Majesty's person, and the persons of the Prince [of Wales] and the Duke of York [James II] out of the hands of those desperate persons who were about them."[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHibbert199365-75) The [Lords Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant "Lord Lieutenant") whom Parliament appointed used the [Militia Ordinance](/wiki/Militia_Ordinance "Militia Ordinance") to order the militia to join Essex's army.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyle2006161,_165-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Rupert_of_the_Rhine.jpg/250px-Rupert_of_the_Rhine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rupert_of_the_Rhine.jpg)

[Prince Rupert of the Rhine](/wiki/Prince_Rupert_of_the_Rhine "Prince Rupert of the Rhine")

Two weeks after the King had raised his standard at Nottingham, Essex led his army north towards [Northampton](/wiki/Northampton "Northampton"),[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970113-77) picking up support along the way (including a detachment of [Huntingdonshire](/wiki/Huntingdonshire "Huntingdonshire") cavalry raised and commanded by Oliver Cromwell).[[e]](#cite_note-79) By mid-September Essex's forces had grown to 21,000 infantry and 4,200 cavalry and [dragoons](/wiki/Dragoon "Dragoon"). On 14 September he moved his army to [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") and then to the north of the [Cotswolds](/wiki/Cotswolds "Cotswolds"),[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970115-80) a strategy that placed it between the Royalists and London. With the size of both armies now in the tens of thousands and only [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") between them, it was inevitable that cavalry reconnaissance units would meet sooner or later. This happened in the first major skirmish of the Civil War, when a troop of about 1,000 Royalist cavalry under [Prince Rupert](/wiki/Prince_Rupert "Prince Rupert"), a German nephew of the King and one of the outstanding cavalry commanders of the war,[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970148-81) defeated a Parliamentary cavalry detachment under Colonel John Brown at the [Battle of Powick Bridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Powick_Bridge "Battle of Powick Bridge"), which crossed the [River Teme](/wiki/River_Teme "River Teme") close to [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England").[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyle2006171–188-82)

Rupert withdrew to Shrewsbury, where a council-of-war discussed two courses of action: whether to advance towards Essex's new position near Worcester, or march down the now open road towards London. The Council decided on the London route, but not to avoid a battle, for the Royalist generals wanted to fight Essex before he grew too strong, and the temper of both sides made it impossible to postpone the decision. In the [Earl of Clarendon's](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon") words, "it was considered more counsellable to march towards London, it being morally sure that the earl of Essex would put himself in their way."[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911404-83) Hence, the army left Shrewsbury on 12 October, gaining two days' start on the enemy, and moved south-east. This had the desired effect of forcing Essex to move to intercept them.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911404-83)

The first [pitched battle](/wiki/Pitched_battle "Pitched battle") of the war, at [Edgehill](/wiki/Battle_of_Edgehill "Battle of Edgehill") on 23 October, proved inconclusive, both Royalists and Parliamentarians claiming victory.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970130–101-84) The second field action, the stand-off at [Turnham Green](/wiki/Turnham_Green_(Battle) "Turnham Green (Battle)"), saw Charles forced to withdraw to [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford"),[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970135-85) which would serve as his base for the rest of the war.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970167–168,_506–507-86)

#### 1643 battles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1643 battles")]

Main article: [First English Civil War, 1643](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War,_1643 "First English Civil War, 1643")

In 1643, Royalist forces won at [Adwalton Moor](/wiki/Battle_of_Adwalton_Moor "Battle of Adwalton Moor"), gaining control of most of [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire").[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970209-87) In the Midlands, a Parliamentary force under [John Gell](/wiki/Sir_John_Gell,_1st_Baronet "Sir John Gell, 1st Baronet") besieged and captured the cathedral city of [Lichfield](/wiki/Lichfield "Lichfield"), after the death of the original commander, [Lord Brooke](/wiki/Robert_Greville,_2nd_Baron_Brooke "Robert Greville, 2nd Baron Brooke").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWanklynJones200574-88) This group then joined forces with [William Brereton](/wiki/Sir_William_Brereton,_1st_Baronet "Sir William Brereton, 1st Baronet") at the inconclusive [Battle of Hopton Heath](/wiki/Battle_of_Hopton_Heath "Battle of Hopton Heath") (19 March), where the Royalist commander, the [Earl of Northampton](/wiki/Spencer_Compton,_2nd_Earl_of_Northampton "Spencer Compton, 2nd Earl of Northampton"), was killed.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWanklynJones200574-88) [John Hampden](/wiki/John_Hampden "John Hampden") died after being wounded in the [Battle of Chalgrove Field](/wiki/Battle_of_Chalgrove_Field "Battle of Chalgrove Field") (18 June 1643).[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdair1976-26) Subsequent battles in the west of England at [Lansdowne](/wiki/Battle_of_Lansdowne "Battle of Lansdowne") and [Roundway Down](/wiki/Battle_of_Roundway_Down "Battle of Roundway Down") also went to the Royalists.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWanklynJones2005103-89) Prince Rupert could then take [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"). In the same year, however, Cromwell formed his troop of "[Ironsides](/wiki/Ironside_(cavalry) "Ironside (cavalry)")", a disciplined unit that demonstrated his military leadership ability. With their assistance he won a victory at the [Battle of Gainsborough](/wiki/Battle_of_Gainsborough "Battle of Gainsborough") in July.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungHolmes1974151-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Alexleslie.jpg/250px-Alexleslie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alexleslie.jpg)

[Alexander Leslie, 1st Earl of Leven](/wiki/Alexander_Leslie,_1st_Earl_of_Leven "Alexander Leslie, 1st Earl of Leven"), Lord General of the Covenanter Army and one of the victors of Marston Moor

At this stage, from 7 to 9 August, there were some popular demonstrations in London – both for and against war. They were protesting at Westminster. A peace demonstration by London women, which turned violent, was suppressed; the women were beaten and fired upon with live ammunition, leaving several dead. Many were arrested and incarcerated in [Bridewell](/wiki/Bridewell "Bridewell") and other prisons.[[86]](#cite_note-91) After these August events, the [Venetian](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") ambassador in England reported to the [doge](/wiki/Doge_of_Venice "Doge of Venice") that the London government took considerable measures to stifle dissent.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorton2011p.~93-92)

In general, the early part of the war went well for the Royalists. The turning point came in the late summer and early autumn of 1643, when the Earl of Essex's army forced the king to raise the [Siege of Gloucester](/wiki/Siege_of_Gloucester "Siege of Gloucester")[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970232-93) and then brushed the Royalists aside at the [First Battle of Newbury](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Newbury "First Battle of Newbury") (20 September),[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970238-94) to return triumphantly to London. Parliamentarian forces led by the Earl of Manchester besieged the port of [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn"), Norfolk, which under [Hamon L'Estrange](/wiki/Hamon_L%27Estrange "Hamon L'Estrange") held out until September.[[90]](#cite_note-95) Other forces won the [Battle of Winceby](/wiki/Battle_of_Winceby "Battle of Winceby"),[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970248-96) giving them control of [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_England "Lincoln, England"). Political manoeuvring to gain an advantage in numbers led Charles to negotiate a ceasefire in Ireland, freeing up English troops to fight on the Royalist side in England,[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970298–299-97) while Parliament offered concessions to the Scots in return for aid and assistance.

#### 1644 battles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 1644 battles")]

Main article: [First English Civil War, 1644](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War,_1644 "First English Civil War, 1644")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Battle_of_Marston_Moor_1644_by_John_Barker.png/500px-Battle_of_Marston_Moor_1644_by_John_Barker.png)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Marston_Moor_1644_by_John_Barker.png)

The [Battle of Marston Moor](/wiki/Battle_of_Marston_Moor "Battle of Marston Moor") in July 1644 was won by the [Parliamentarians](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead")

Helped by the Scots, Parliament won at [Marston Moor](/wiki/Battle_of_Marston_Moor "Battle of Marston Moor") (2 July),[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWanklynJones2005189-98) gaining [York](/wiki/York "York") and the north of England.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970322-99) Cromwell's conduct in the battle proved decisive,[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970319-100) and showed his potential as a political and as an important military leader. Parliament's defeat at the [Battle of Lostwithiel](/wiki/Battle_of_Lostwithiel "Battle of Lostwithiel") in Cornwall, however, marked a serious reverse in the south-west of England.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshley1990188-101) Subsequent [fighting around Newbury](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Newbury "Second Battle of Newbury") (27 October), though tactically indecisive, strategically gave another check to Parliament.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970359-102)

#### 1645 battles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 1645 battles")]

Main article: [First English Civil War, 1645](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War,_1645 "First English Civil War, 1645")

In 1645, Parliament reaffirmed its determination to fight the war to a finish. It passed the [Self-denying Ordinance](/wiki/Self-denying_Ordinance "Self-denying Ordinance"), by which all members of either House of Parliament laid down their commands and re-organised its main forces into the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army"), under the command of [Thomas Fairfax](/wiki/Thomas_Fairfax "Thomas Fairfax"), with Cromwell as his second-in-command and [Lieutenant-General](/wiki/Lieutenant_general "Lieutenant general") of Horse.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970373-103) In two decisive engagements – the [Battle of Naseby](/wiki/Battle_of_Naseby "Battle of Naseby") on 14 June and the [Battle of Langport](/wiki/Battle_of_Langport "Battle of Langport") on 10 July – the Parliamentarians effectively destroyed Charles's armies.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970428-104)

#### 1646 battles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 1646 battles")]

Main article: [First English Civil War, 1646](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War,_1646 "First English Civil War, 1646")

In the remains of his English realm, Charles tried to recover a stable base of support by consolidating the Midlands. He began to form an axis between Oxford and [Newark-on-Trent](/wiki/Newark-on-Trent "Newark-on-Trent") in Nottinghamshire. These towns had become fortresses and showed more reliable loyalty to him than others. He took [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester "Leicester"), which lies between them, but found his resources exhausted. Having little opportunity to replenish them, in May 1646 [he sought shelter](/wiki/Charles_I%27s_journey_from_Oxford_to_the_Scottish_army_camp_near_Newark "Charles I's journey from Oxford to the Scottish army camp near Newark") with a Presbyterian Scottish army at [Southwell](/wiki/Southwell,_Nottinghamshire "Southwell, Nottinghamshire") in Nottinghamshire.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970519–520-105) Charles was eventually handed over to the English Parliament by the Scots and imprisoned.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWedgwood1970570-106) This marked the end of the First English Civil War.

### Interbellum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Interbellum")]

See also: [Putney Debates](/wiki/Putney_Debates "Putney Debates") and [Levellers](/wiki/Levellers "Levellers")

The end of the [First Civil War](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War "First English Civil War"), in 1646, left a partial power vacuum in which any combination of the three English factions, Royalists, [Independents](/wiki/Independents_(religion) "Independents (religion)") of the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army") ("the Army"), and [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") of the English Parliament, as well as the Scottish Parliament allied with the Scottish Presbyterians (the "[Kirk](/wiki/Kirk_(word) "Kirk (word)")"), could prove strong enough to dominate the rest. Armed political [Royalism](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") was at an end, but despite being a prisoner, Charles I was considered by himself and his opponents (almost to the last) as necessary to ensure the success of whichever group could come to terms with him. Thus, he passed successively into the hands of the Scots, the Parliament and the Army.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAtkinson191145._Second_Civil_War_(1648–52)-107)

The King attempted to reverse the verdict of arms by "[coquetting](/wiki/Flirting "Flirting")" with each in turn. On 3 June 1647, Cornet [George Joyce](/wiki/George_Joyce "George Joyce") of Thomas Fairfax's cavalry seized the King for the New Model Army; following the seizure, the English Presbyterians and the Scots began to prepare for a fresh civil war, less than two years after the conclusion of the first, this time against "Independency" as embodied in the Army. After making use of the Army, its opponents attempted to disband it, send it onward to foreign service, and to cut off its arrears of pay.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAtkinson191145._Second_Civil_War_(1648–52)-107)

The result was that the Army leadership was exasperated beyond control, and, remembering not merely its grievances but also the principle for which the Army had fought, it soon became the most powerful political force in the realm. From 1646 to 1648 the breach between Army and Parliament widened day by day, until finally the Presbyterian party, combined with the Scots and the remaining Royalists, felt itself strong enough to begin a Second Civil War.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAtkinson191145._Second_Civil_War_(1648–52)-107)

### Second English Civil War (1648–1649)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Second English Civil War (1648–1649)")]

Main article: [Second English Civil War](/wiki/Second_English_Civil_War "Second English Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/William_Frederick_Yeames_-_And_when_did_you_last_see_your_father%3F_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/500px-William_Frederick_Yeames_-_And_when_did_you_last_see_your_father%3F_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Frederick_Yeames_-_And_when_did_you_last_see_your_father%3F_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

"And when did you last see your father?" by [William Frederick Yeames](/wiki/William_Frederick_Yeames "William Frederick Yeames"). The oil-on-canvas picture, painted in 1878, depicts a scene in an imaginary Royalist household during the English Civil War. The Parliamentarians have taken over the house and question the son about his Royalist father. The man lounging on a chair in the centre of the scene is identifiable as a [Roundhead](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") officer by his military attire and his orange sash.

Charles I took advantage of the deflection of attention away from himself to negotiate on 28 December 1647 a secret treaty with the Scots, again promising church reform.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeel199964-108) Under the agreement, called the "[Engagement](/wiki/Engagers "Engagers")", the Scots undertook to invade England on Charles's behalf and restore him to the throne.[[104]](#cite_note-109)

A series of Royalist uprisings throughout England and a Scottish invasion occurred in the summer of 1648. Forces loyal to Parliament[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFairfax1648Letter-110) put down most of those in England after little more than a skirmish, but uprisings in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex") and [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland "Cumberland"), the rebellion in Wales, and the Scottish invasion involved pitched battles and prolonged sieges.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeel199964-108)

In the spring of 1649, unpaid Parliamentarian troops in Wales changed sides. Colonel [Thomas Horton](/wiki/Thomas_Horton_(soldier) "Thomas Horton (soldier)") defeated the Royalist rebels at the [Battle of St Fagans](/wiki/Battle_of_St_Fagans "Battle of St Fagans") (8 May)[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohn2008127-111) and the rebel leaders surrendered to Cromwell on 11 July after a protracted two-month [siege of Pembroke](/wiki/Siege_of_Pembroke "Siege of Pembroke").[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrevelyan2002274-112) Thomas Fairfax defeated a Royalist uprising in Kent at the [Battle of Maidstone](/wiki/Battle_of_Maidstone "Battle of Maidstone") on 1 June. Fairfax, after his success at [Maidstone](/wiki/Maidstone "Maidstone") and the pacification of Kent, turned north to reduce Essex, where, under an ardent, experienced and popular leader, [Charles Lucas](/wiki/Charles_Lucas "Charles Lucas"), the Royalists had taken up arms in great numbers. Fairfax soon drove the enemy into [Colchester](/wiki/Colchester "Colchester"), but his first attack on the town met with a repulse and he had to settle down to [a long siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Colchester "Siege of Colchester").[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrevelyan2002274–275-113)

In the North of England, Major-General [John Lambert](/wiki/John_Lambert_(General) "John Lambert (General)") fought a successful campaign against several Royalist uprisings, the largest being that of [Marmaduke Langdale](/wiki/Marmaduke_Langdale,_1st_Baron_Langdale_of_Holme "Marmaduke Langdale, 1st Baron Langdale of Holme") in Cumberland.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman200687-114) Thanks to Lambert's successes, the Scottish commander, the [Duke of Hamilton](/wiki/James_Hamilton,_3rd_Marquess_and_1st_Duke_of_Hamilton "James Hamilton, 3rd Marquess and 1st Duke of Hamilton"), had to take a western route through [Carlisle](/wiki/Carlisle,_Cumbria "Carlisle, Cumbria") in his pro-Royalist [Scottish invasion of England](/wiki/Scottish_invasion_of_England_(1648) "Scottish invasion of England (1648)").[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman200689-115) The Parliamentarians under Cromwell engaged the Scots at the [Battle of Preston](/wiki/Battle_of_Preston_(1648) "Battle of Preston (1648)") (17–19 August). The battle took place largely at [Walton-le-Dale](/wiki/Walton-le-Dale "Walton-le-Dale") near [Preston, Lancashire](/wiki/Preston,_Lancashire "Preston, Lancashire"), and resulted in a victory for Cromwell's troops over the Royalists and Scots commanded by Hamilton.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman200689-115) This victory marked the end of the Second English Civil War.

Nearly all the Royalists who had fought in the First Civil War had given their word not to bear arms against Parliament, and many, like [Lord Astley](/wiki/Jacob_Astley,_1st_Baron_Astley_of_Reading "Jacob Astley, 1st Baron Astley of Reading"), were therefore bound by oath not to take any part in the second conflict. So, the victors in the Second Civil War showed little mercy to those who had brought war into the land again. On the evening of the surrender of Colchester, Parliamentarians had [Charles Lucas](/wiki/Charles_Lucas "Charles Lucas") and [George Lisle](/wiki/George_Lisle_(Royalist) "George Lisle (Royalist)") shot.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrevelyan2002275-116) Parliamentary authorities sentenced the leaders of the Welsh rebels, Major-General [Rowland Laugharne](/wiki/Rowland_Laugharne "Rowland Laugharne"), Colonel [John Poyer](/wiki/John_Poyer "John Poyer") and Colonel Rice Powel to death, but executed only Poyer (25 April 1649), having selected him by lot.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGardiner200646-117) Of five prominent Royalist peers who had fallen into Parliamentary hands, three – the Duke of Hamilton, the [Henry Rich, Earl of Holland](/wiki/Henry_Rich,_1st_Earl_of_Holland "Henry Rich, 1st Earl of Holland"), and [Arthur Capell](/wiki/Arthur_Capell,_1st_Baron_Capell_of_Hadham "Arthur Capell, 1st Baron Capell of Hadham"), one of the Colchester prisoners and a man of high character – were beheaded at Westminster on 9 March.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGardiner200612-118)

### Trial of Charles I for treason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Trial of Charles I for treason")]

Main articles: [High Court of Justice for the trial of Charles I](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice_for_the_trial_of_Charles_I "High Court of Justice for the trial of Charles I") and [Execution of Charles I](/wiki/Execution_of_Charles_I "Execution of Charles I")

Charles's secret pacts and encouragement of supporters to break their parole caused Parliament to debate whether to return the King to power at all. Those who still supported Charles's place on the throne, such as the army leader and moderate Fairfax, tried again to negotiate with him.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAylmer198023-119) The Army, furious that Parliament continued to countenance Charles as a ruler, then marched on Parliament and conducted "[Pride's Purge](/wiki/Pride%27s_Purge "Pride's Purge")", named after the commanding officer of the operation, [Thomas Pride](/wiki/Thomas_Pride "Thomas Pride"), in December 1648.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAylmer198022-120)

Troops arrested 45 members and kept 146 out of the chamber. They allowed only 75 members in, and then only at the Army's bidding. This [Rump Parliament](/wiki/Rump_Parliament "Rump Parliament") received orders to set up, in the name of the people of England, a [High Court of Justice for the trial of Charles I](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice_for_the_trial_of_Charles_I "High Court of Justice for the trial of Charles I") for treason.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAylmer198025-121) Fairfax, a constitutional monarchist, declined to have anything to do with the trial. He resigned as head of the army, so clearing Cromwell's road to power.

At the end of the trial the [59 Commissioners](/wiki/List_of_regicides_of_Charles_I "List of regicides of Charles I") judged Charles I guilty of [high treason](/wiki/High_treason "High treason") as a "tyrant, traitor, murderer and public enemy".[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelsey2003583–616-122)[[118]](#cite_note-123) He was [beheaded](/wiki/Beheading "Beheading") on a scaffold in front of the [Banqueting House](/wiki/Banqueting_House "Banqueting House") of the [Palace of Whitehall](/wiki/Palace_of_Whitehall "Palace of Whitehall") on 30 January 1649.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStoyle2011"Overview:_Civil_War_and_Revolution,_1603–1714"-124) After the [Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration") in 1660, nine of the surviving [regicides](/wiki/List_of_regicides_of_Charles_I "List of regicides of Charles I") not living in exile were executed and most others sentenced to life imprisonment.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKirby199925-125)

After the regicide, [Charles, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") as the eldest son was publicly proclaimed King Charles II in the Royal Square of [St. Helier](/wiki/Saint_Helier "Saint Helier"), [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey"), on 17 February 1649 (after a first such proclamation in Edinburgh on 5 February 1649). It took longer for the news to reach the trans-Atlantic colonies, with the Somers Isles (also known as [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda")) becoming the first to proclaim Charles II King on 5 July 1649.[[121]](#cite_note-Lefroy-126)

### Third English Civil War (1649–1651)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Third English Civil War (1649–1651)")]

#### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Ireland")]

Main article: [Anglo-Scottish war (1650–1652)](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_war_(1650%E2%80%931652) "Anglo-Scottish war (1650–1652)")

See also: [Cromwellian conquest of Ireland](/wiki/Cromwellian_conquest_of_Ireland "Cromwellian conquest of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Massacre_at_Drogheda.jpeg/330px-Massacre_at_Drogheda.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Massacre_at_Drogheda.jpeg)

A 19th-century representation of the Massacre at [Drogheda](/wiki/Drogheda "Drogheda") in Ireland in 1649

Ireland had undergone continual war since the [rebellion of 1641](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1641 "Irish Rebellion of 1641"), with most of the island controlled by the [Irish Confederates](/wiki/Confederate_Ireland "Confederate Ireland").[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeniham2008121-127) Increasingly threatened by the armies of the English Parliament after Charles I's arrest in 1648, the Confederates signed a treaty of alliance with the English Royalists.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeniham2008122-128) The joint Royalist and Confederate forces under [James Butler, Duke of Ormonde](/wiki/James_Butler,_1st_Duke_of_Ormonde "James Butler, 1st Duke of Ormonde") tried to eliminate the Parliamentary army holding [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") by [laying siege in 1649](/wiki/Siege_of_Dublin_(1649) "Siege of Dublin (1649)"), but their opponents routed them at the [Battle of Rathmines](/wiki/Battle_of_Rathmines "Battle of Rathmines") (2 August 1649).[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeniham2008127-129) [Admiral Robert Blake](/wiki/Admiral_Robert_Blake "Admiral Robert Blake"), a former Member of Parliament, had blockaded Prince Rupert's fleet in [Kinsale](/wiki/Kinsale "Kinsale"), enabling Oliver Cromwell to land at [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") on 15 August 1649 with an army to quell the Royalist alliance.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeniham2008128-130)

Cromwell's suppression of the Royalists in Ireland in 1649 is still remembered by many Irish people. After the [Siege of Drogheda](/wiki/Siege_of_Drogheda "Siege of Drogheda"),[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeniham2008128-130) the massacre of nearly 3,500 people – around 2,700 Royalist soldiers and 700 others, including civilians, prisoners, and Catholic priests (all of whom Cromwell claimed had carried arms) – became one of the historical memories that has driven Irish-English and Catholic-Protestant strife during the last three centuries. The Parliamentarian conquest of Ireland ground on for another four years until 1653, when the last [Irish Confederate](/wiki/Confederate_Ireland "Confederate Ireland") and Royalist troops surrendered.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeniham2008132-131) In the wake of the conquest, the victors confiscated almost all Irish Catholic-owned land and distributed it to Parliament's creditors, to Parliamentary soldiers who served in Ireland, and to English who had settled there before the war.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeniham2008135–136-132)

#### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Scotland")]

See also: [Scotland in the Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Scotland in the Wars of the Three Kingdoms")

The execution of [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") altered the dynamics of [the Civil War in Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Scotland in the Wars of the Three Kingdoms"), which had raged between Royalists and [Covenanters](/wiki/Covenanter "Covenanter") since 1644. By 1649, the struggle had left the Royalists there in disarray and their erstwhile leader, [James Graham, Marquess of Montrose](/wiki/James_Graham,_1st_Marquess_of_Montrose "James Graham, 1st Marquess of Montrose"), had gone into exile. At first, [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") encouraged Montrose to raise a Highland army to fight on the Royalist side.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarpenter2005145-133) When the Scottish Covenanters, who did not agree with the execution of Charles I and who feared for the future of [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") under the new [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England"), offered him the crown of Scotland, Charles abandoned Montrose to his enemies.

Montrose, who had raised a [mercenary](/wiki/Mercenary "Mercenary") force in Norway,[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarpenter2005145-133) had already landed and could not abandon the fight. He did not succeed in raising many Highland clans and the Covenanters defeated his army at the [Battle of Carbisdale](/wiki/Battle_of_Carbisdale_1650 "Battle of Carbisdale 1650") in [Ross-shire](/wiki/Ross-shire "Ross-shire") on 27 April 1650. The victors captured Montrose shortly afterwards and took him to Edinburgh. On 20 May the Scottish Parliament sentenced him to death and had him hanged the next day.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarpenter2005146-134)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Cromwell_at_Dunbar_Andrew_Carrick_Gow.jpg/250px-Cromwell_at_Dunbar_Andrew_Carrick_Gow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cromwell_at_Dunbar_Andrew_Carrick_Gow.jpg)

*[Cromwell at Dunbar](/wiki/Cromwell_at_Dunbar "Cromwell at Dunbar")*, by [Andrew Carrick Gow](/wiki/Andrew_Carrick_Gow "Andrew Carrick Gow"), 1886

Charles II landed in Scotland at [Garmouth](/wiki/Garmouth "Garmouth") in [Morayshire](/wiki/Morayshire "Morayshire") on 23 June 1650[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrett200839-135) and signed the 1638 [National Covenant](/wiki/National_Covenant "National Covenant") and the 1643 [Solemn League and Covenant](/wiki/Solemn_League_and_Covenant "Solemn League and Covenant") shortly after coming ashore.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrett200841-136) With his original Scottish Royalist followers and his new Covenanter allies, Charles II became the greatest threat facing the new English republic. In response to the threat, Cromwell left some of his lieutenants in Ireland to continue the suppression of the Irish Royalists and returned to England.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarpenter2005146-134)

He arrived in Scotland on 22 July 1650[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReidTurner200418-137) and proceeded to lay siege to Edinburgh. By the end of August, disease and a shortage of supplies had reduced his army, and he had to order a retreat towards his base at [Dunbar](/wiki/Dunbar "Dunbar"). A Scottish army under the command of [David Leslie](/wiki/David_Leslie_(Scottish_general) "David Leslie (Scottish general)") tried to block the retreat, but Cromwell defeated them at the [Battle of Dunbar](/wiki/Battle_of_Dunbar_(1650) "Battle of Dunbar (1650)") on 3 September. Cromwell's army then took Edinburgh, and by the end of the year his army had occupied much of southern Scotland.

In July 1651, Cromwell's forces crossed the [Firth of Forth](/wiki/Firth_of_Forth "Firth of Forth") into [Fife](/wiki/Fife "Fife") and defeated the Scots at the [Battle of Inverkeithing](/wiki/Battle_of_Inverkeithing "Battle of Inverkeithing") (20 July 1651).[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarpenter2005158-138) The New Model Army advanced towards [Perth](/wiki/Perth,_Scotland "Perth, Scotland"), which allowed Charles, at the head of the Scottish army, to move south into England. Cromwell followed Charles into England, leaving [George Monck](/wiki/George_Monck "George Monck") to finish the campaign in Scotland. Monck took [Stirling](/wiki/Stirling "Stirling") on 14 August and [Dundee](/wiki/Dundee "Dundee") on 1 September.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarpenter2005185-139) The next year, 1652, saw a mopping up of the remnants of Royalist resistance, and under the terms of the "[Tender of Union](/wiki/Tender_of_Union "Tender of Union")", the Scots received 30 seats in a united Parliament in London, with General Monck as the military governor of Scotland.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDand197220-140)

#### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: England")]

Although Cromwell's New Model Army had defeated a Scottish army at Dunbar, Cromwell could not prevent Charles II from marching from Scotland deep into England at the head of another Royalist army.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeiser20031-141) They marched to the west of England where English Royalist sympathies were strongest, but although some English Royalists joined the army, they were far fewer in number than Charles and his Scottish supporters had hoped. Cromwell finally engaged and defeated the new Scottish king at [Worcester](/wiki/Battle_of_Worcester "Battle of Worcester") on 3 September 1651.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarpenter2005145-133)[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAtkin2008[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_September_2016]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (September_2016)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-142)

#### Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Wales")]

For several reasons most of [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") was not as engaged in the English Civil Wars to the same degree as other parts of the British Isles. Wales was isolated from England, both physically and linguistically, so the Welsh were not as much engaged as England in the issues between the king and Parliament.[[138]](#cite_note-143) The English considered Wales a remote land, with Welsh, not English, as the primary language. Since England had formally assimilated Wales into the kingdom, starting in 1536 formal agreements had been put in place under Henry VIII and continued under Charles I that allowed for Welsh local administrative authority and economic control, which allowed the Welsh to function to some degree independently.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1987488-144)[[140]](#cite_note-Stoyle-145)[[141]](#cite_note-146) Another factor was the Puritan religion, which played a major role in the English Civil Wars but was not widely practised throughout Wales. Welsh Puritan religious dominance was found in northeast Wales near [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham "Wrexham"), [Denbighshire](/wiki/Denbighshire "Denbighshire"), and an indirect Puritan influence found along the southwestern coast near [Haverfordwest](/wiki/Haverfordwest "Haverfordwest"), Pembroke, and [Tenby](/wiki/Tenby "Tenby") due to a combination of a strong influence by the third earl of Essex and their strong trade relations with Bristol, England, a fervent Puritan stronghold.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins19876-147)[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies1993276-148) In addition, Wales was comparatively more rural in character than England at this time, and lacked the large number of urban settlements home to mercantile, trade, and manufacturing interests who were a bulwark of support for both Puritanism and eventually the Parliamentarian cause.

Many of the key Welsh Civil Wars leaders were from the gentry class holding Royalist sympathies,[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1987488-144) or from the Church. Those Welsh who did participate in the Civil Wars battles were underequipped, underfed, and not properly trained for warfare.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins198725-149) The majority of Welsh followed the Protestant faith with a religious perspective that differed from the English puritan zeal.[[140]](#cite_note-Stoyle-145) They were also leery of the Irish Catholics invading Wales. The Welsh also did not want to lose what they had, for the gentry were aware of the destruction the Thirty Years' War caused in Europe.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1987489-150)

Most of those English Civil War battles where Wales was impacted occurred near the border with England and in south Wales.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins198728–29-151) Some of the more significant engagements were:

* In Gloucester, England (not far from Wales) Lord Herbert of Raglan (Wales) had Welsh troops assisting the royalists trying to take Gloucester in March, August, and September 1643, but without success;[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins198710-152)
* In November 1643 [Thomas Myddelton](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_Myddelton,_1st_Baronet "Sir Thomas Myddelton, 1st Baronet") had secured the north Wales Royalist stronghold of Flintshire and the area east of Denbighshire, depriving Royalists based in Chester, England of their supplies.
