In response to this attack Archbishop John Williams, on behalf of the Royalists, responded to this attack by taking Wrexham from the Parliamentarians;[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins198710–12-153)
* Initially in the summer of 1643, Royalist forces under [Richard Vaughan of Golden Grove, 2nd Earl of Carbery](/wiki/Richard_Vaughan,_2nd_Earl_of_Carbery "Richard Vaughan, 2nd Earl of Carbery"), who had been appointed lieutenant-general by the King, was successful in securing three of the southwestern Welsh counties; but in early 1644 Parliamentarians conducted a successful sea and land assault campaign on Pembroke, Haverfordwest, Milford Pil; and continuing on to Swansea and Cardiff. As a result of these Royalist failures the King replaced Carbery with Colonel [Charles Gerard, 1st Earl of Macclesfield](/wiki/Charles_Gerard,_1st_Earl_of_Macclesfield "Charles Gerard, 1st Earl of Macclesfield") who was able to regain many of these lost territories in Cardiganshire and Carmarthenshire;[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins198712–13-154)
* On 18 September 1644 the first pitched battle on Welsh soil at Montgomery was a successful win for Myddelton;[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins198714–15-155)
* On 1 August 1645 the Royalist forces were once again defeated at Colby Moor;[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins198715–16-156)
* During the Second Civil War the Royalists were decisively defeated at the battle of St. [Fagans](/wiki/Battle_of_St_Fagans "Battle of St Fagans") near Cardiff, which was one of the last more significant battles.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins198721-157)

In addition to the Civil Wars' impact on the monarchy and the changes in national leadership, unexpected outcomes of the English Civil Wars to Wales included a significant degradation of the country's road system, a deterioration of government administrative functions to the general population, destruction of castles with only the remnants of them remaining, and the desecration of churches.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJenkins198728–29-151)

#### Immediate aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Immediate aftermath")]

After the Royalist defeat at Worcester, [Charles II escaped](/wiki/Escape_of_Charles_II "Escape of Charles II") to France via safe houses and [an oak tree](/wiki/Royal_Oak_(tree) "Royal Oak (tree)").[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeiser20031-141) Parliament was left in *de facto* control of England. Resistance continued for a time in Ireland and Scotland, but with the pacification of England, resistance elsewhere did not threaten the military supremacy of the New Model Army and its Parliamentary paymasters.

## Political control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Political control")]

During the Wars, the Parliamentarians established a number of successive committees to oversee the war effort. The first, the [Committee of Safety](/wiki/English_Committee_of_Safety "English Committee of Safety") set up in July 1642, comprised 15 members of Parliament.[[153]](#cite_note-158) After the Anglo-Scottish alliance against the Royalists, the [Committee of Both Kingdoms](/wiki/Committee_of_Both_Kingdoms "Committee of Both Kingdoms") replaced the Committee of Safety between 1644 and 1648.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy200096-159) Parliament dissolved the Committee of Both Kingdoms when the alliance ended, but its English members continued to meet as the [Derby House Committee](/wiki/Derby_House_Committee "Derby House Committee").[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy200096-159) A second Committee of Safety then replaced it.

### Episcopacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Episcopacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/William_Laud.jpg/250px-William_Laud.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Laud.jpg)

[William Laud](/wiki/William_Laud "William Laud"), Charles I's Archbishop of Canterbury.

During the English Civil War, the role of bishops as wielders of political power and upholders of the [established church](/wiki/Established_church "Established church") became a matter of heated political controversy. [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") of [Geneva](/wiki/Calvin%27s_Geneva "Calvin's Geneva") had formulated a doctrine of [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), which held that the offices of *presbyter* and *episkopos* in the New Testament were identical; he rejected the more traditional doctrine of [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession"). Calvin's follower [John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox") brought Presbyterianism to Scotland when the Scottish church was reformed in 1560. In practice, Presbyterianism meant that committees of lay elders had a substantial voice in church government, as opposed to merely being subjects to a ruling hierarchy.

This vision of at least partial democracy in [ecclesiology](/wiki/Ecclesiology "Ecclesiology") paralleled the struggles between Parliament and the King. A body within the Puritan movement in the Church of England sought to abolish the office of bishop and remake the Church of England along Presbyterian lines. The [Martin Marprelate](/wiki/Martin_Marprelate "Martin Marprelate") tracts (1588–1589), applying the [pejorative](/wiki/Pejorative "Pejorative") name of *[prelacy](/wiki/Prelacy "Prelacy")* to the church hierarchy, attacked the office of bishop with satire that deeply offended [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") and her [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury") [John Whitgift](/wiki/John_Whitgift "John Whitgift"). The [vestments controversy](/wiki/Vestments_controversy "Vestments controversy") also related to this movement, seeking further reductions in church ceremony, and labelling the use of elaborate vestments as "unedifying" and even [idolatrous](/wiki/Idolatry_and_Christianity "Idolatry and Christianity").

[King James VI](/wiki/James_I_of_England "James I of England"), reacting against the perceived [contumacy](/wiki/Contumacy "Contumacy") of his Presbyterian Scottish subjects, adopted "No Bishop, no King" as a slogan. He tied the hierarchical authority of the bishop to the absolute authority he sought as King and viewed attacks on the authority of the bishops as attacks on his authority. Matters came to a head when Charles I appointed [William Laud](/wiki/William_Laud "William Laud") as [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"). Laud aggressively attacked the Presbyterian movement and sought to impose the full [Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer"). The controversy eventually led to Laud's impeachment for [treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason") by a [bill of attainder](/wiki/Bill_of_attainder "Bill of attainder") in 1645 and subsequent execution. Charles also attempted to impose episcopacy on Scotland. The Scots' violent rejection of bishops and liturgical worship sparked the [Bishops' Wars](/wiki/Bishops%27_Wars "Bishops' Wars") in 1639–1640.

During the height of Puritan power under [the Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") and [the Protectorate](/wiki/The_Protectorate "The Protectorate"), episcopacy was formally abolished in the Church of England on 9 October 1646.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKing1968523–537-160) The Church of England remained Presbyterian until the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration") of the monarchy.

Further information: [English overseas possessions in the Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/English_overseas_possessions_in_the_Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "English overseas possessions in the Wars of the Three Kingdoms")

During the English Civil War, the English overseas possessions became highly involved. In the Channel Islands, the island of Jersey and [Castle Cornet](/wiki/Castle_Cornet "Castle Cornet") in Guernsey supported the King until a surrender with honour in December 1651.

Although the newer, Puritan settlements in North America, notably Massachusetts (which still styles itself the *"[Commonwealth of Massachusetts](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Massachusetts "Commonwealth of Massachusetts")"*), were dominated by Parliamentarians, the older colonies sided with the Crown. Friction between Royalists and Puritans in Maryland came to a head in the [Battle of the Severn](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Severn "Battle of the Severn"). The [Virginia Company's](/wiki/Virginia_Company_of_London "Virginia Company of London") settlements, [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") and [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), as well as [Antigua](/wiki/Antigua "Antigua") and [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"), were conspicuous in their loyalty to the Crown. Bermuda's Independent Puritans were expelled, settling the [Bahamas](/wiki/Bahamas "Bahamas") under [William Sayle](/wiki/William_Sayle "William Sayle") as the [Eleutheran Adventurers](/wiki/Eleutheran_Adventurers "Eleutheran Adventurers"). Parliament passed [An Act for prohibiting Trade with the Barbadoes, Virginia, Bermuda and Antego](/wiki/An_Act_for_prohibiting_Trade_with_the_Barbadoes,_Virginia,_Bermuda_and_Antego "An Act for prohibiting Trade with the Barbadoes, Virginia, Bermuda and Antego") in October 1650, which stated that:

> ...due punishment [be] inflicted upon the said Delinquents, do Declare all and every the said persons in Barbada's, Antego, Bermuda's and Virginia, that have contrived, abetted, aided or assisted those horrid Rebellions, or have since willingly joyned with them, to be notorious Robbers and Traitors, and such as by the Law of Nations are not to be permitted any manner of Commerce or Traffic with any people whatsoever; and do forbid to all manner of persons, Foreigners, and others, all manner of Commerce, Traffic and Correspondence whatsoever, to be used or held with the said Rebels in the Barbados, Bermuda's, Virginia and Antego, or either of them.[[156]](#cite_note-161)

The Act also authorised Parliamentary [privateers](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") to act against English vessels trading with the rebellious colonies:

> All Ships that Trade with the Rebels may be surprized. Goods and tackle of such ships not to be embezeled, till judgement in the Admiralty.; Two or three of the Officers of every ship to be examined upon oath.

Far to the North, Bermuda's regiment of Militia and its coastal batteries prepared to resist an invasion that never came.[[157]](#cite_note-162)[[158]](#cite_note-163) Built-up inside the natural defence of a nearly impassable barrier reef, to fend off the might of Spain, these defences would have been a formidable obstacle for the Parliamentary fleet sent in 1651 under the command of Admiral [George Ayscue](/wiki/George_Ayscue "George Ayscue") to subdue the trans-Atlantic colonies, but after the fall of Barbados, the Bermudians made a separate peace that respected the internal status quo. The [Parliament of Bermuda](/wiki/Parliament_of_Bermuda "Parliament of Bermuda") avoided the Parliament of England's fate during [The Protectorate](/wiki/The_Protectorate "The Protectorate"), becoming one of the oldest continuous legislatures in the world.[[121]](#cite_note-Lefroy-126)[[159]](#cite_note-164)[[160]](#cite_note-165)

Virginia's population swelled with Cavaliers during and after the English Civil War. Even so, Virginia Puritan [Richard Bennett](/wiki/Richard_Bennett_(Governor) "Richard Bennett (Governor)") was made Governor answering to Cromwell in 1652, followed by two more nominal "Commonwealth Governors". The loyalty of Virginia's [Cavaliers](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_(historical) "Virginia Cavaliers (historical)") to the Crown was rewarded after the 1660 Restoration of the Monarchy when Charles II dubbed it the *Old Dominion*.

## Casualties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Casualties")]

Figures for casualties during this period are unreliable. Some attempt has been made to provide rough estimates.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1992[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidtVugNXnVrVACpgPP10_pp._211–214]-166)

In England, a conservative estimate is that roughly 100,000 people died from war-related disease during the three civil wars, plus at least 60,000 in Scotland. Historical records count 84,830 combat dead from the wars themselves.[[1]](#cite_note-Clod-1) Counting in accidents and the two Bishops' wars, a "conservative" estimate of 190,000 dead is achieved,[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1992[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidtVugNXnVrVACpgPP10_p._211]-167) out of a total population of about five million.[[163]](#cite_note-James-187-168) It is estimated that from 1638 to 1651, from a pre-war population of approximately 6 million (5 million in England and Wales and 1 million in Scotland)[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1995214-169) some 25% of all adult males in Britain served in the military at some point. Deaths amounted to 3.7 per cent of the total population in England and 6 per cent in Scotland.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1995340-170)

As was typical for the era, most combat deaths occurred in minor skirmishes rather than large, pitched battles. There were a total of 645 engagements throughout the wars: 588 of these involved fewer than 250 casualties in total, with these 588 accounting for 39,838 fatalities (average count of less than 68) or nearly half of the conflict's combat deaths. There were only 9 major pitched battles (at least 1,000 fatalities) which in total accounted for 15% of casualties.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1995206-171)

An anecdotal example of how high casualties in England may have been perceived is to be found in the posthumously published writing (generally titled *The History of Myddle*), by a [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire") man, Richard Gough (lived 1635–1723) of [Myddle](/wiki/Myddle "Myddle") near [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Shrewsbury "Shrewsbury"), who, writing in about 1701, commented of men from his rural home parish who joined the Royalist forces: "And out of these three townes [*sic* - ie [townships](/wiki/Township_(England) "Township (England)")], Myddle, Marton and Newton, there went noe less than twenty men, of which number thirteen were kill'd in the warrs".[[167]](#cite_note-172) After listing those he recalled did not return home, four of whose exact fates were unknown, he concluded: "And if soe many dyed out of these 3 townes [townships] wee may reasonably guess that many thousands dyed in England in that warre."[[168]](#cite_note-173)

Figures for Scotland are less reliable and should be treated with caution. Casualties include the deaths of prisoners-of-war in conditions that accelerated their deaths, with estimates of 10,000 prisoners not surviving or not returning home (8,000 captured during and immediately after the [Battle of Worcester](/wiki/Battle_of_Worcester "Battle of Worcester") were deported to [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") and the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies") to work for landowners as [indentured labourers](/wiki/Indentured_labour "Indentured labour")[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyle2006602-174)). There are no figures to calculate how many died from war-related diseases, but if the same ratio of disease to battle deaths from English figures is applied to the Scottish figures, a not unreasonable estimate of 60,000 people is achieved,[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1992[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidtVugNXnVrVACpgPP10_p._212]-175) from a population of about one million.[[163]](#cite_note-James-187-168)

Figures for Ireland are described as "miracles of conjecture". Certainly, the devastation inflicted on Ireland was massive, with the best estimate provided by [William Petty](/wiki/William_Petty "William Petty"), the father of English demography. Petty estimated that 112,000 Protestants and 504,000 Catholics were killed through [plague](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), war and [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine"), giving an estimated total of 616,000 dead,[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlton1992[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidtVugNXnVrVACpgPP10_p._213]-176) out of a pre-war population of about one and a half million.[[163]](#cite_note-James-187-168) Although Petty's figures are the best available, they are still acknowledged as tentative; they do not include an estimated 40,000 driven into exile, some of whom served as soldiers in European continental armies, while others were sold as indentured servants to New England and the West Indies. Many of those sold to landowners in New England eventually prospered, but many sold to landowners in the West Indies were worked to death.

These estimates indicate that England suffered a 4 per cent loss of population, Scotland a loss of 6 per cent, while Ireland suffered a loss of 41 per cent of its population. Putting these numbers into the context of other catastrophes helps to understand the devastation of Ireland in particular. The [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") of 1845–1852 resulted in a loss of 16 per cent of the population, while during the Soviet famine and [Holodomor](/wiki/Holodomor "Holodomor") of 1932–33 the population of the Soviet Ukraine fell by 14 per cent.[[172]](#cite_note-Conquest-177)

## Popular gains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Popular gains")]

Ordinary people took advantage of the dislocation of civil society in the 1640s to gain personal advantages. The contemporary guild democracy movement won its greatest successes among London's transport workers. The old *status quo* began a retrenchment after the end of the First Civil War in 1646, and more especially after the Restoration in 1660, but some gains were long-term. The democratic element introduced into the [Watermen's Company](/wiki/Watermen%27s_Company "Watermen's Company") in 1642, for example, survived with vicissitudes until 1827.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindley1997160-178)

Parliament adopted a policy of confiscating ("sequestrating") the estates of "delinquents" (active Royalists) in 1643, a policy carried out by local committees.[[174]](#cite_note-179) Rural communities seized timber and other resources on the sequestrated estates of Royalists and Catholics, and on the estates of the royal family and church hierarchy. Some communities improved their conditions of tenure on such estates.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Riordan1993184–200-180)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

The wars left England, Scotland, and Ireland among the few countries in Europe without a monarch. In the wake of victory, many of the ideals (and many idealists) became sidelined. The republican government of the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") ruled England (and later all of Scotland and Ireland) from 1649 to 1653 and from 1659 to 1660. Between the two periods, and due to in-fighting among various factions in Parliament, [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") ruled over [the Protectorate](/wiki/The_Protectorate "The Protectorate") as [Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector") (effectively a military dictator) until his death in 1658.[[f]](#cite_note-181)

On Oliver Cromwell's death, his son [Richard](/wiki/Richard_Cromwell "Richard Cromwell") became Lord Protector, but the Army had little confidence in him.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeble20026-182) After seven months the Army removed Richard. In May 1659 it re-installed the Rump.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeble20029-183) Military force shortly afterward dissolved this as well.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeble200212-184) After the second dissolution of the Rump, in October 1659, the prospect of a total descent into [anarchy](/wiki/Anarchy "Anarchy") loomed, as the Army's pretense of unity dissolved into factions.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeble200234-185)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Musket_volley_by_Sealed_Knot.JPG/500px-Musket_volley_by_Sealed_Knot.JPG)](/wiki/File:Musket_volley_by_Sealed_Knot.JPG)

Muskets being fired at a civil war re-enactment

Into this atmosphere General [George Monck](/wiki/George_Monck,_1st_Duke_of_Albemarle "George Monck, 1st Duke of Albemarle"), Governor of Scotland under the Cromwells, marched south with his army from Scotland. On 4 April 1660, in the [Declaration of Breda](/wiki/Declaration_of_Breda "Declaration of Breda"), Charles II made known the conditions of his acceptance of the Crown of England.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeble200231-186) Monck organised the [Convention Parliament](/wiki/Convention_Parliament_(1660) "Convention Parliament (1660)"),[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeble200248-187) which met for the first time on 25 April 1660.

On 8 May 1660, it declared that Charles II had reigned as the lawful monarch since the execution of Charles I in January 1649. Charles returned from exile on 23 May 1660. On 29 May 1660, the populace in London acclaimed him as king.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELodge20075–6-188) His coronation took place at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") on 23 April 1661. These events became known as the *[Restoration](/wiki/Restoration_(England) "Restoration (England)")*.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELodge20076-189)

Although the monarchy was restored, it was still with the consent of Parliament. So the civil wars effectively set England and Scotland on course towards a [parliamentary monarchy](/wiki/Parliamentary_monarchy "Parliamentary monarchy") form of government.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELodge20078-190) The outcome of this system was that the future [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), formed in 1707 under the [Acts of Union](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1707 "Act of Union 1707"), managed to forestall the kind of revolution typical of European republican movements which generally resulted in total abolition of their monarchies. Thus, the United Kingdom was spared the wave of revolutions that occurred in Europe in the 1840s. Specifically, future monarchs became wary of pushing Parliament too hard, and Parliament effectively chose the line of royal succession in 1688 with the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution").

### Hobbes' *Behemoth*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Hobbes' Behemoth")]

[Thomas Hobbes](/wiki/Thomas_Hobbes "Thomas Hobbes") gave an early historical account of the English Civil War in his *[Behemoth](/wiki/Behemoth_(Hobbes_book) "Behemoth (Hobbes book)")*, written in 1668 and published in 1681. He assessed the causes of the war to be the conflicting political doctrines of the time.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHobbes1839220-191) *Behemoth* offered a uniquely historical and philosophical approach to naming the catalysts for the war. It also attempted to explain why Charles I could not hold his throne and maintain peace in his kingdom.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKraynak199033-192)

Hobbes analysed the following aspects of English thought during the war: the opinions of divinity and politics that spurred rebellion; rhetoric and doctrine used by the rebels against the king; and how opinions about "taxation, the conscription of soldiers, and military strategy" affected the outcomes of battles and shifts of sovereignty.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKraynak199033-192)

Hobbes attributed the war to the novel theories of intellectuals and divines spread for their own pride of reputation.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldsmith1966x–xiii-193) He held that clerical pretensions had contributed significantly to the troubles — "whether those of puritan fundamentalists, papal supremacists or divine right Episcopalians".[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommerville2012-194) Hobbes wanted to abolish the independence of the clergy and bring it under the control of the civil state.

Some scholars suggest that Hobbes's *Behemoth* has not received its due as an academic work, being comparatively overlooked and under-rated in the shadow of the same author's *[Leviathan](/wiki/Leviathan_(Hobbes_book) "Leviathan (Hobbes book)")*.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKraynak1990-195)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacgillivray1970179-196) Its scholarly reputation may have suffered because it takes the form of a dialogue, which, while common in philosophy, is rarely adopted by historians. Other factors that hindered its success include Charles II's refusing its publication and Hobbes' lack of empathy with views different from his own.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacgillivray1970179-196)

### Whig and Marxist views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Whig and Marxist views")]

In the early decades of the 20th century, the [Whig school](/wiki/Whig_history "Whig history") was the dominant theoretical view. It explained the Civil War as resulting from centuries of struggle between Parliament (notably the House of Commons) and the Monarchy, with Parliament defending the traditional rights of Englishmen, while the Stuart monarchy continually attempted to expand its right to dictate law arbitrarily. The major Whig historian, [S. R. Gardiner](/wiki/Samuel_Rawson_Gardiner "Samuel Rawson Gardiner"), popularised the idea that the English Civil War was a "Puritan Revolution"[[191]](#cite_note-197) that challenged the repressive Stuart Church and prepared the way for [religious toleration](/wiki/Religious_toleration "Religious toleration"). Thus, Puritanism was seen as the natural ally of a people preserving their traditional rights against arbitrary monarchical power.

The Whig view was challenged and largely superseded by the [Marxist](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism") school, which became popular in the 1940s, and saw the English Civil War as a [bourgeois](/wiki/Bourgeois "Bourgeois") revolution. According to Marxist historian [Christopher Hill](/wiki/Christopher_Hill_(historian) "Christopher Hill (historian)"):

> The Civil War was a class war, in which the despotism of Charles I was defended by the reactionary forces of the established Church and conservative landlords, Parliament beat the King because it could appeal to the enthusiastic support of the trading and industrial classes in town and countryside, to the yeomen and progressive gentry, and to wider masses of the population whenever they were able by free discussion to understand what the struggle was really about.[[192]](#cite_note-198)

### Later views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Later views")]

In the 1970s, [revisionist historians](/wiki/Historical_revisionism "Historical revisionism") challenged both the Whig and the Marxist theories,[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess1990609–627-199) notably in the 1973 anthology *The Origins of the English Civil War* ([Conrad Russell](/wiki/Conrad_Russell "Conrad Russell") ed.).[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussell1973[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_April_2017]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (April_2017)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-200) These historians focused on the minutiae of the years immediately before the civil war, returning to the contingency-based historiography of [Clarendon's](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon") *History of the Rebellion and Civil Wars in England*.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaunt200060-201) This, it was claimed, demonstrated that patterns of war allegiance did not fit either Whig or Marxist theories.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaunt200060-201) Parliament was not inherently progressive, nor the events of 1640 a precursor for the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution").[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaunt200060–61-202) Many members of the bourgeoisie fought for the King, while many landed aristocrats supported Parliament.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess1990609–627-199)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

From the 1990s, a number of historians replaced the historical title "English Civil War" with "[Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms")" and "British Civil Wars", positing that the civil war in England cannot be understood apart from events in other parts of Britain and Ireland. King Charles I remains crucial, not just as King of England, but through his relationship with the peoples of his other realms. For example, the wars began when Charles forced an Anglican Prayer Book upon Scotland, and when this was met with resistance from the [Covenanters](/wiki/Covenanter "Covenanter"), he needed an army to impose his will. However, this need of military funds forced Charles I to call an English Parliament, which was not willing to grant the needed revenue unless he addressed their grievances.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOhlmeyer2002-203)

By the early 1640s, Charles was left in a state of near-permanent crisis management, confounded by the demands of the various factions. For example, Charles finally made terms with the Covenanters in August 1641, but although this might have weakened the position of the English Parliament, the [Irish Rebellion of 1641](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1641 "Irish Rebellion of 1641") broke out in October 1641, largely negating the political advantage he had obtained by relieving himself of the cost of the Scottish invasion.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOhlmeyer2002-203)

A number of revisionist historians such as [William M. Lamont](/wiki/William_M._Lamont "William M. Lamont") regarded the conflict as a religious war, with [John Morrill](/wiki/John_Morrill_(historian) "John Morrill (historian)") (1993) stating: 'The English Civil War was not the first European revolution: it was the last of the Wars of Religion.'[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess1998175-204) This view has been criticised by various pre-, post- and anti-revisionist historians.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess1998175-204) Glen Burgess (1998) examined political propaganda written by the Parliamentarian politicians and clerics at the time, noting that many were or may have been motivated by their Puritan religious beliefs to support the war against the 'Catholic' king Charles I, but tried to express and legitimise their opposition and rebellion in terms of a legal revolt against a monarch who had violated crucial constitutional principles and thus had to be overthrown.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess1998196–197-205) They even warned their Parliamentarian allies to not make overt use of religious arguments in making their case for war against the king.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess1998196–197-205)

However, in some cases it may be argued that they hid their pro-Anglican and anti-Catholic motives behind legal parlance, for example by emphasising that the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") was the *legally established* religion: 'Seen in this light, the defences of Parliament's war, with their apparent legal-constitutional thrust, are not at all ways of saying that the struggle was not religious. On the contrary, they are ways of saying that it was.'[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess1998198–200-206) Burgess concluded: '[T]he Civil War left behind it just the sort of evidence that we could reasonably expect a war of religion to leave.'[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess1998201-207)

## Re-enactments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_Civil_War&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Re-enactments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Civil_war_reeanactment.JPG/500px-Civil_war_reeanactment.JPG)](/wiki/File:Civil_war_reeanactment.JPG)

Pikes deployed at a civil war re-enactment

Two large historical societies exist, [The Sealed Knot](/wiki/The_Sealed_Knot_(reenactment) "The Sealed Knot (reenactment)") and [The English Civil War Society](/wiki/The_English_Civil_War_Society "The English Civil War Society"), which regularly [re-enact](/wiki/English_Civil_War_reenactment "English Civil War reenactment") events and battles of the Civil War in full period costume.
